posts.text:
Dear Giacomo,
I ran simply with combine -t -1 datacard.txt -m 5.36688  and output is:
<<< Combine >>>
<<< v10.0.2 >>>
>>> Random number generator seed is 123456
>>> Method used is AsymptoticLimits

-- AsymptoticLimits ( CLs ) --
Observed Limit: r < 441.8823

Done in 0.04 min (cpu), 0.04 min (real)

Which doesn’t have any statistical uncertainity.
When I give some more parameters in input:
combine -t -1 nocat_datacard.txt -m 5.36688 --X-rtd REMOVE_CONSTANT_ZERO_POINT=1 --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0 --cminDefaultMinimizerTolerance=0.0001  --expectSignal=0
Then output shows:
<<< Combine >>>
<<< v10.0.2 >>>

Random number generator seed is 123456
Method used is AsymptoticLimits

-- AsymptoticLimits ( CLs ) --
Observed Limit: r < 141.0363
Expected  2.5%: r < 531.1085
Expected 16.0%: r < 532.6830
Expected 50.0%: r < 537.4062
Expected 84.0%: r < 548.1169
Expected 97.5%: r < 562.9653

Done in 0.01 min (cpu), 0.01 min (real)

Which shows a complete mis-match observed and expected limits.
Thanks and regards, Sanjeev

posts.name: Philip Keicher
posts.text:
Hi @sakumar ,
the issue is probably that you use a different set of options in the commands that you shared above. Since this generally has an impact on the exact minimization process in the background, it’s not really surprising that you results also change.
Generally, it’s always a good idea to use one consistent set of options if possible. See also the documentation on the Asymptotic limits routine - for example, it’s not recommended to use the options -t -1 with that, but rather the --run option (more information in the previous link).
Additionally, I’m not sure why you would expect the observed and expected datasets to be the same - it’s two different datasets afterall. Can you explain this expectation a little more?
Cheers,
Philip

posts.name: Giacomo Ortona
posts.text:
Hi @sakumar,
On top of what @pkeicher just said, the reason you don’t get any expected results with your first command is that you should set a reasonable range for your signal strength. The default range is [0:20], way too small to accomodate your case.
Something like --rMin 100 --rMax 600 should do the trick, judging from your output. The algorithm is more tolerant against this range for the observed result, and some other options like --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0 also can work around it, that’s why you get the output of the second command, but the best strategy is always to identify a proper range and use that.
Finally, I’m not sure you need the --X-rtd REMOVE_CONSTANT_ZERO_POINT option. This is usually intended for discrete profiling, which does not appear to be your case.
Best regards,
Giacomo

posts.name: Giacomo Ortona
posts.text:
Dear @sakumar ,
I was taking a deeper look at your workspaces, and it seems like the background function you are using is not continuous, but has a hole in the region [4.8,5.8], can you please check if this is true? Did you blind the background function instead of the data? Moreover, it also seems to depend on the signal via a fsig variable. Are you sure this is an intended behaviour?
I can’t tell for sure, but I doubt the asymptotic formula will give meaningful results in these conditions, so if this setup is intended I would encourage you to try with toy-based limit computations and see if those are more stable.
Cheers,
Giacomo

Dear Giacomo,
I tried toy based limit computations as described at https://cms-analysis.github.io/HiggsAnalysis-CombinedLimit/part5/longexercise/#advanced-section-b-computing-limits-with-toys
It gives me following warning message:
FASTEXIT from pdf_binTag0_obsOnly
WARNING: underflow to nan in pdf_binTag0_obsOnly for bin 0, weight 1

I tried to check which pdf bin has nan in it. But could not find it.
I also tried to give unblinded input (as you mentioned in previous email). But could not get any difference. Updated workspace is kept at https://github.com/sanjeevphys/bph/blob/master/combine/nocat_datacard.root
Thanks and regards, Sanjeev

posts.text:
Dear Sanjeev,
Sorry I’m still going through the mails arrived during the holidays.
If you don’t get any value it means the fit failed. Running with a verbose mode (-v 5) should give you more details about when/where this happens.
Are the datacards still in the same folder?
Cheers,
Giacomo

Giacomo Ortona gortonaDecember 19, 2024

Dear @sakumar ,
I was taking a deeper look at your workspaces, and it seems like the background function you are using is not continuous, but has a hole in the region [4.8,5.8], can you please check if this is true? Did you blind the background function instead of the data? Moreover, it also seems to depend on the signal via a fsig variable. Are you sure this is an intended behaviour?
I can’t tell for sure, but I doubt the asymptotic formula will give meaningful results in these conditions, so if this setup is intended I would encourage you to try with toy-based limit computations and see if those are more stable.
Cheers,
Giacomo

posts.name: Giacomo Ortona
posts.text:
Dear Sanjeev,
I’m still running more test, but my guess is that you have some correlation between the parameters of the signal and background model, and the fit isn’t able to resolve the ambiguity. One possible cause is that you have more parameters than your (blind) data is able to resolve, so what I believe is happening is that you end up in a situation where it is always possible for the fit to play with the parameters of the model and fit the results, so it can’t find an actual minimum.
This is all a bit of speculation on my side, but for example I was able to obtain a result by freezing the background parameters and constraining a bit more the signal ones:
combine nocat_datacard.root --run blind -m 5.36688 --freezeParameters=p_{0},tau,f --rMin 0 --rMax 100000 --setParameterRanges sigma_{L}=0,1:#alpha_{R}=-1,1:#alpha_{L}=-1,1
A similar outcome (with different numbers of course) can be obtained by freezing most of the signal parameters.
Can you check if this does make sense? And if so, are there any model parameters you can freeze safely beforehand?
Best regards,
Giacomo
